questions.question: Assertion (A): The length of the tangent drawn from an external point to a circle is always equal to the radius of the circle. Reason (R): The tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.

steps: The length of a tangent is independent of the radius length.
The theorem states that a tangent is perpendicular to the radius at the point of contact.

questions.question: Assertion (A): To construct a pair of tangents to a circle from an external point, the angle between the tangents is supplementary to the angle subtended by the line segment joining the points of contact at the center. Reason (R): The sum of opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral is $180^\circ$.

steps: The quadrilateral formed by the center, two contact points, and the external point has two right angles.
The sum of angles is $360^\circ$, so the remaining two must sum to $180^\circ$.
solution.derivation: A: True, R: True, R explains A

questions.question: Assertion (A): It is impossible to construct a tangent to a circle from a point lying inside the circle. Reason (R): A tangent touches the circle at only one point.

steps: A line passing through an interior point will always be a secant, not a tangent.
A tangent by definition meets the circle at exactly one point on the circumference.
solution.derivation: A: True, R: True, R explains A

questions.question: Assertion (A): The number of tangents that can be drawn from a point on the circle to the circle is exactly two. Reason (R): A point on the circle lies on the circumference.

steps: Only one tangent can be drawn from a point on the circle.
The reason is a true statement about the location of the point.

questions.question: Assertion (A): When constructing tangents to a circle from an external point $P$, we bisect the line segment $OP$ where $O$ is the center. Reason (R): The perpendicular bisector of $OP$ helps in finding the midpoint which acts as the center of a circle passing through $O$ and $P$.

steps: To find points of contact, we draw a circle with diameter $OP$.
The midpoint is found by constructing the perpendicular bisector of $OP$.
solution.derivation: A: True, R: True, R explains A
